data.title: Expect Less In 2026 - But Don't Bet Against CGDV
data.description:
The Capital Group Dividend Value ETF (CGDV) is a core portfolio candidate, and neither a true dividend nor a true value proposition. This is a filtered version of SPY with an emphasis on quality. This blended (more tilted value, but decently growth oriented within the large cap group) active strategy has been ahead of the broader markets for a few years now. So its recent outperformance against SPY does not raise eyebrows, as did the case of MGV I recently write about. CGDV's thesis is not about value versus growth ahead - it never has been. CGDV's Buy thesis rests on growth in quality large caps. That should hold up well in 2026 and relative performance against SPY is likely to stay intact. However, we may see a more subdued year overall in absolute return terms.

Methodology and Portfolio Clarifications

CGDV's name suggests dividend value. The website shows the weighted average portfolio more accurately classified as blended, but still shows a tilt toward value. The truth is that dividend-paying stocks make up the core of the portfolio and serve only as a gating variable check for entry - the quality focus likely stops there. The large cap bias is also evident and adds to the quality focus versus SPY.

Valuation metrics do not show expected value characteristics. The P/E discount versus SPY is modest (~8%). The P/CF ratio is only slightly higher than the S&P 500. The P/B ratio reflects a discount but that is likely the effect of profitability focus rather than valuations. CGDV is not definitely low multiple investing - it is slightly cheaper than SPY (potentially classifiable as GARP) but still very much a growth strategy at heart.

The portfolio holds most of the tech mega-caps that are in the top 10 SPY Holdings, but the allocations hold less weight. CGDV underweights Amazon, Apple, Alphabet and Nvidia significantly and reallocates weights to select growth infrastructure names like Broadcom, Applied Materials and GE. It also weeds out speculative growth names (like Tesla) to a large extent. The overall portfolio is heavily concentrated. The top 10 have a similar ~36% combined weight. However, the portfolio size (number of holdings) is almost a tenth of SPY, implying narrower exposure and a missing tail in lower market cap names.

CGDV achieves its growth tilt without similar emphasis in terms of weight on tech. The tech quality has been conducive though with greater emphasis on AI themes and semis and less exposure to software. Unlike traditional value-focused strategies, CGDV is dramatically underweight financials. The real tilt versus SPY is in industrials, energy, healthcare and material - sectors that have also seen secular growth in the current cycle, especially if right selection calls were made.

Overall, CGDV, unlike its name suggests, is a SPY peer with a quality tilt. Income is part of the story, and more likely a selection criterion proxying for quality. Growth in CGDV thrives when quality mega caps win over speculative growth. This is a core portfolio allocation.

Performance and Reasons

I look at CGDV's historical performance to understand the core drivers and trends that can be extrapolated ahead. First of all, CGDV's outperformance versus SPY is not a recent development. There has been a sustained performance gap since 2023, only enhanced in the bull run post April 2025 and still continuing as broader markets have consolidated in the past 3-4 months.

This structural and persistent gap comes from the portfolio. We have discussed some aspect of that in the previous section. Such as the bet on quality tech and a lack of software and speculative tech growth names that have worked. CGDV has accumulated its alpha through other sector positioning too. The overweight healthcare and industrials positioning has allowed it to capture growth from GLP-1 leadership trends, infrastructure spending tailwinds and defense and aerospace strength. Energy as a theme has also seen growth in pockets due to electrification and AI demand - something SPY has not been able to capture as much. The underweight in financials means less drag that dilutes growth elsewhere as has potentially happened in the case of SPY.

The Road Ahead

So that brings us to the critical point around what to expect in 2026. Let us break that down into two sub-questions. The first question to be answered is absolute expectations and the second is around its relative performance versus SPY.

The relative outperformance scenario is still supportive for CGDV, which is why I rate it a Buy. CGDV needs AI leadership to be broad at the top - that is narrative-based smaller caps should not outperform and at the same time AI mega caps at the top don't concentrate growth in 1-2 names SPY is overweight on. The second outcome cannot be ruled out entirely, and a couple of mega caps could support SPY over CGDV, but if growth is more spread out across the Magnificent 7, CGDV should be able to capture some of that growth.

In terms of vertical segments, semis and hardware over software should remain the theme and that is likely supported because of two factors that are already playing out. The software sector has undergone severe corrections already and could mechanically rebound a bit. However, I do not see much structural growth there because the question is around how AI disrupts the space and commoditizes some of the offerings and reduces pricing power. That answer will likely need time to be answered, so software will await long term clarity over headline metrics. At the same time, AI hardware and infra, especially semis, will remain supported in the near term because of the historically elevated capex spend levels we are seeing. That actually dampens the free cash flow of those in the AI race, but means business for the infrastructure layer. CGDV is well positioned in terms of both these outlooks.

The industrials allocation should also remain strong, if not deliver further alpha in 2026. Defense spending should be strong, given the geopolitical tensions in play. The only area we need to keep a watch on is PMI improvements and steady capital goods demand - not a concern right now.

In healthcare, we should continue to see relative strength in GLP-1 momentum, even if biotech risk appetite does not rebound strongly in an environment where long duration risk appetite is visibly slowing. The energy theme also looks relatively good if oil prices remain above marginal cost of production and if there is no deep global recession. The odds of recession in the US have meaningfully compressed from ~60% odds in 2025 to ~40% odds - not a non-issue, but a lower probability outcome. Energy is also well-supported due to AI power demand tailwinds.

Other factors that could have tilted the total return outlook in favor of SPY like a broader market rally (smaller caps) is unlikely to happen unless rates fall meaningfully and liquidity rises, or economic growth rebounds strongly - not a scenario expected in 2026. Overall, the markets will likely continue to favor quality premium and CGDV should hold its lead.

However, there is the other important sub-question around absolute growth expectations. I expect CGDV to have a significantly flatter year in 2026 (mid-single digit outlook) than the rise in 2025. And this is because of the fact that growth investing is currently cooling and is likely to remain so until earnings begin to see upgrade momentum - something that is not happening today. And unlike value plays like MGV, which I recently wrote about, CGDV does not have rotation trends to rely on to make for an outsized return thesis, despite a slower growth environment.

The summary therefore reads like this. CGDV is likely to keep its lead over SPY ahead. It is a Buy both in relative and absolute terms. However, the absolute growth expectations should be subdued compared to its recent past - a Buy with tempered expectations.

data.title: AI Buildout Will Keep The Broad Bull Market Intact
data.description:
The last several months have been exceedingly frustrating ones for stock market bears, who have strained their patience waiting for a “correction” in the major indexes that has thus far eluded them. As measured by the S&P 500 index, the market has, up until now, refused to comply with the bears’ expectations—even with the latest flare-up in the Middle East.

Granted that a market decline could occur at any time in the present environment, my contention is that any setback in the broad U.S. equity in the coming days and weeks is likely to be very short-lived. That’s the case I’ll attempt to prove out here, and I believe the weight of evidence supports it. And while the evidence I’ll cite is partly technical in nature, it ultimately revolves around the ongoing artificial intelligence buildout being the main support for keeping stock prices elevated across several major industries.

To begin with, let’s take a look at what I consider the most salient aspects of the current bull market. First, the key components include the financial and tech sectors; specifically, banks, broker/dealers, and semiconductors.

The importance of these sectors is clear enough, with the banks and broker/dealers serving as barometers of financial sector strength, as well as the magnitude of liquidity in the market (which is one of the most important considerations for the broad market’s overall health). As for the semiconductor stocks, this group is not only an excellent reflection of the health of the overall tech sector, but I think it’s also safe to say that chip stock strength reflects the progression of the AI buildout.

Despite interest rate volatility, tariff-related angst, and geopolitical worries, U.S. bank and broker/dealer stocks have held up very admirably so far this year. Consider the KBW Bank Index (BKX), which many analysts view as the prime benchmark for the bank stocks in the aggregate.

It’s true that BKX has shown some weakness over the last few days, as reflected in the chart below, but its main trend has been strong since the last major correction almost a year ago. And while a test of the widely watched 200-day moving average (the red line in the chart shown here) is indeed a strong possibility in the coming days, even if this trend line is broken, I don’t envision the bank stocks remaining under prolonged selling pressure.

Underpinning this view is the mostly sanguine view for 2026 expressed among banking executives at a recent Piper Sandler-sponsored financial services conference. According to the presenters at this conference (held in November), the banking sector is expected to outperform this year compared to 2025 due to a variety of trends, including improved credit. Quoting from a recently published overview of the conference:

Bank management teams are positioning constructively for 2026, citing disruption-driven hiring, diminishing commercial real estate lending drag, stable to upward trending margins, a favorable regulatory environment, and robust capital return dynamics as key factors supporting their outlook.”

The article further stated that Piper Sandler viewed core credit trends as remaining “extremely strong…with banks well-reserved and well-capitalized, with actual loss content not matching recent negative speculation.”

On the subject of the improving credit outlook, it’s also worth noting that liquidity has been very strong so far this year, thanks in large part to the Federal Reserve’s recent bond-buying operations. Specifically, the Fed has been steadily buying U.S. Treasuries—about $40 billion per month—prompting some Fed watchers to call it “QE5” (and to be fair, the Fed isn’t calling it quantitative easing).

But as the Fed’s bond-buying operations are expected to continue until at least mid-April, I believe this will continue to keep the major stock market indexes buoyant through the spring, since it provides an extra injection of liquidity (the lifeblood of any bull market).

As far as the impact the Iran/Israel war is having on the equity market, the venerable Dr. Ed Yardeni was interviewed this week on Bloomberg and stated his opinion that “for all practical purposes, this war is over for Iran.” He also expressed his view that the market is already looking beyond this geopolitical event and may be viewing it as potentially increasing stability in the Middle East, with stocks likely having already discounted this outcome.

Turning to the semiconductors, the PHLX Semiconductor Index (SOX) presents an even better picture of where the broad market currently stands. My observation has always been that “as go the semis, so goes the Nasdaq, so goes the broad market.” In other words, SOX strength typically translates into categorical strength due to the outsized importance of this particular industry.

Unlike the bank stocks, chip stocks, as reflected by the SOX chart (below), are very close to their yearly highs and well above the 200-day moving average—and in spite of increased investor anxiety over the semiconductor stocks at large.

With semiconductors more important today than ever as it pertains to the AI and data center buildouts, the momentum behind the semis (at least in my estimation) is the fact that chip demand remains robust as the buildout trend continues. On that score, Nvidia’s (NVDA) recent blowout earnings report and massive fiscal Q1 revenue guidance have been interpreted by industry analysts as “the AI chip boom is still accelerating.”

In terms of potential risk to my thesis, if the current Iran/Israel/U.S. conflict isn’t resolved in a timely fashion and extends beyond the four-to-five-week timeframe to victory the White House and other military experts are projecting right now, there could be extended volatility in the energy markets. This in turn could exert a headwind on stocks and keep the major indexes from making progress in the coming months; it could even contribute to sustained selling pressure in equities.

Also, a failure of the Fed to continue its present measures of providing additional liquidity to the market beyond the projected mid-April period (in terms of its bond-buying operations) might serve to deter robust participation from institutional investors, with the possible result that the bears would find it easier to successfully “raid” the more vulnerable sectors of the market.

That said, I believe the combination of 1.) a worst-case scenario of the latest Middle East flare-up likely already discounted by the market, 2.) abundant liquidity thanks to the Fed’s bond-buying operations, 3.) an improved banking and credit outlook for 2026, and 4.) ongoing data center and AI buildout strength as reflected in the semiconductor stock will contribute to ongoing strength in the broad market.

As such, I’m planning to stay fully invested in equities regardless of any near-term volatility that might rear its head in the coming days. I see the broad market’s uptrend remaining intact heading into the spring months, with the bears once again having to exercise long patience for their day to arrive.

data.title: Wall Street Lunch: Kevin Warsh's Job Just Got Harder
data.description:
Listen below or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Bond market signals rising inflation worries ahead of Kevin Warsh’s Fed chairmanship. (0:15) ISM services index jumps to three-year high. (1:09) Crypto-linked stocks rally after Trump urges banks to work with industry. (1:55)

The following is an abridged transcript:

Fed-chief-to-be Kevin Warsh’s job is getting tougher by the day.

The White House may want lower fed funds rates, but the bond market is signaling rising inflation worries after the attacks on Iran and the surge in oil prices.

Trump has said the conflict could last four to five weeks.

That leaves a couple of months for things to normalize before Warsh’s first meeting — though Polymarket odds currently predict a cessation of hostilities by the end of June.

Fed funds futures now show a 60% chance rates stay unchanged after Warsh’s inaugural meeting — up from 54% a week ago and 42% a month ago. Two quarter-point cuts are still priced in by year-end, though the odds have dipped to around 60%.

Minneapolis Fed President Neel Kashkari said it’s “too soon to know” what impact a war with Iran might have on inflation or how long any effects could last.

“Coming into this moment, I was feeling pretty good,”he said. “I felt like policy was in a pretty good place.”

On the economic front, the ISM Services Index jumped to 56.1 in February from 53.8 in January, beating the 53.5 consensus.

It’s the highest level in more than three years and marks the 20th-straight month of expansion.

Economists at Wells Fargo say some service-sector firms are adding headcount in anticipation of stronger activity.

And while higher prices remain a burden, the ISM data suggest cost pressures aren’t getting materially worse. Their bottom line: service-sector conditions are improving as businesses navigate steep uncertainty.

And ahead of Friday’s jobs report, ADP says private-sector employment grew by 63K in February — topping the 43K consensus.

That’s roughly in line with what economists expect from the official numbers, though ADP’s track record is spotty.

Among active stocks, crypto exchange names — including Coinbase Global (COIN), Galaxy Digital (GLXY), bitcoin miners, and other crypto-linked stocks — rallied after President Donald Trump urged banks to “make a good deal” with the crypto industry to advance stalled legislation.

A major sticking point for progress on the crypto bill remains Wall Street’s objection to stablecoins that feature yield-like payments.

CrowdStrike (CRWD) is choppy post-earnings, but it got plenty of love from Wall Street.

RBC’s Matthew Hedberg said the company “delivered a very strong quarter by accelerating at scale and raising FY27 ARR expectations.”

He added that even with the raised outlook, “upside is likely throughout the year.”

And Moderna (MRNA) continued to rise as analysts said its agreement to resolve a patent dispute with Arbutus Biopharma (ABUS) and Roivant’s Genevant unit (ROIV) removed a key legal overhang for the COVID vaccine maker.

Moderna agreed to pay $950M to settle the dispute related to delivery technology behind its vaccine.

In other news of note, whale to the rescue.

Billionaire Leo KoGuan, one of the largest individual shareholders of Tesla (TSLA), said he bought 1M shares of Nvidia (NVDA).

“I am convinced AI is NOT a bubble — it is only the beginning,” he posted.

He told Bloomberg he plans to buy another 1M Nvidia shares soon “to show support to a nervous market.”

And in the Wall Street Research Corner, Brown Brothers Harriman says institutional investors are preparing to expand their exposure to exchange traded funds — even as market volatility pushes portfolio managers to refocus on strategies aimed at generating alpha.

In the firm’s Global ETF Investor Survey, 96% of respondents said they plan to increase ETF allocations over the next 12 months — matching last year’s record level.

“ETF issuers are innovating to meet the evolving needs of today’s investors,” said Tim Huver, managing director on ETF Servicing.

He added that the next phase of growth will likely emphasize active management, broader asset-class access, and more sophisticated operational capabilities.

data.title: Gold's Pullback During Global Turmoil Does Not Change The Long-Term Bull Thesis
data.description:
When geopolitical tensions erupt, as they have this week, investors reflexively flock to safe havens, with gold (GLD) leading the charge. However, this week's escalating conflict between the US, Israel, and Iran counterintuitively witnessed selling in gold and Treasury bonds, while the US Dollar and crude oil surged. To some, this price action indicates that gold has exhausted its safe haven status and may be vulnerable to a deeper correction. However, interpreting this drop as the end of gold's massive bull run misreads the mechanics that are driving prices in the short term and underestimates the continuing structural shift in global finance that appears to be strengthening.

Gold's recent weakness is not a rejection of its safe-haven status; instead, it is a mechanical reaction to an overbought market that was blindsided and a sudden increase in the market's expectation for real yields. Because surging oil prices act as an immediate inflationary tax, investors are betting that the Federal Reserve will be handicapped in lowering interest rates later this year, making gold less attractive at the margin relative to holding cash.

While these short-term headwinds are real, they do not negate the long-term thesis for gold that has strengthened due to the conflict. The gold bull market strengthened in 2022 when the US froze Russia's access to SWIFT in response to the war in Ukraine. This led to a structural change in how Central Banks (CBs) view US Dollar reserves, sparking a historic wave of gold accumulation. If reserves in US Dollars can be frozen, then CBs reasoned that they needed to diversify their holdings, favoring gold. Ironically, the current aggressive 'go-it-alone' US policy only reinforces this view in the long term. While rising oil prices may delay Fed rate cuts, the accelerating global shift toward a more multipolar world ensures that the structural underpinning of gold's bull market remains very much intact.

The Geopolitical Shock in Iran and Current Market Disconnect

The joint US and Israeli military campaign in Iran that commenced over the weekend sent immediate shock waves through global markets; however, the market's reaction defied the typical geopolitical crisis playbook.

The chart below shows the short-term reaction to this new development. Crude oil surged, as seen by WTI, Brent and the United States Oil Fund (USO). Gold (GLD) initially followed suit on Monday but sold off sharply on Tuesday. Similarly, the S&P 500 (SPY) surprisingly brushed off geopolitical concerns on Monday but traded sharply lower on Tuesday. Finally, Treasury bonds also traded lower, as seen by the price action of the iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (TLT). The price swing in long-term Treasury bonds was also mirrored by the overall bond market (BND). Finally, the US Dollar surged, as seen by the performance of the US Dollar Index Bullish Fund (UUP).

A cursory look at the chart above indicates that the only safe havens in the current crisis are oil and the US Dollar. While a worsening situation in Iran could continue this short-term trend, it is important to note that gold is not being rejected as a safe haven. Like US Treasury bonds, it is pricing in a sudden shift in monetary policy expectations.

The Short-Term Headwind: Oil, The Fed and Real Yields

Coming into this week, the rally in gold prices was partly premised on the market's expectation for a falling Fed Funds Rate in 2026. As inflation has generally moderated, investors have been pricing in lower rates by the end of the year.

This repricing is visible in the chart below: while the current forecast remains below where it was four weeks ago, expectations have increased noticeably higher compared to just one or two weeks ago. Because surging oil prices are inflationary, investors are betting that the chances for more rate cuts in 2026 are worse than one week ago. Now market participants see a Fed Funds rate of ~3.2% by year-end, as pain at the pump may handicap the Fed's ability to cut interest rates.

Because gold provides no yield, a primary source of competition is cash. The higher the real interest rate from holding cash, the more demand for gold is siphoned into the dollar. This dynamic perfectly explains the sudden Tuesday sell-off: as the oil shock hit, the bond market aggressively priced out near-term Fed dovishness, triggering a mechanical rotation out of zero-yielding gold and into higher-yielding short-term cash. This move was exacerbated by the recently surging gold price, as overbought conditions are more susceptible to swift corrections.

The Structural Shift Toward a More Indebted, Multipolar World

There are three primary factors that drive the long-term bull thesis on gold, and all have strengthened this week. First, over the past 20 years, the money supply has greatly expanded, as seen by the US M2 money supply. Since the Great Recession in 2008, M2 has roughly tripled.

The Federal Deficit has also spiked. Preceding the 2008 crisis, the US government ran deficits that were approximately 2% of GDP. However, this spiked to 10% of GDP in 2010 and 14% of GDP following the COVID shutdown in 2020.

Cumulatively, total Federal Debt has increased from $8.37T in Q1 2006 to approximately $38.5T today, a 4.6x rise. Gold has risen even more, from $570/oz in Jan 2006 to over $5100/oz today.

While it could be argued that gold has more than discounted the rising US debt, there is another important factor to consider. In 2022, the bull market in gold entered a new paradigm as the US and its allies froze Russia's foreign exchange reserves by denying Russia access to the SWIFT messaging network.

Suddenly, global CBs realized that holding US Dollars carried the risk that the US could cut them off. This realization sparked a historic wave of gold accumulation by CBs. Immediately following 2022, worldwide CB purchases of gold skyrocketed.

The current conflict in Iran acts as a "SWIFT 2.0" for observing nations. The US acting in an aggressive and unilateral manner reinforces the urgent need for non-aligned countries to shift from Dollar reserves to gold reserves.

Institutions like the People's Bank of China, the National Bank of Poland, and the Reserve Bank of India are not buying gold to trade the federal funds rate; they are buying it as a mandatory national security asset. The United States' action in Iran serves to reinforce this demand while simultaneously increasing the Federal Deficit due to increased military spending.

Conclusion

While gold has shown technical weakness due to increasing interest rate expectations, this seems unlikely to end the bull market. In fact, the US acting unilaterally in Iran reinforces a multipolar worldview that will increase demand for gold as central banks continue their historic pace of accumulation. This persistent buying cements demand, strengthening gold's appeal rather than reversing it. Further, the structural indebtedness of the United States is increasing, compounded by the Supreme Court ruling against tariff revenues and rising military expenditures.

Therefore, investors looking for a safe haven in a multipolar, indebted world should look past the cyclical trend toward higher interest rates and instead focus on the secular trends benefiting gold. Similar to the 1970s, the real threat to gold is prudent austerity and high interest rates to subdue inflation. With these remedies far off, the bull market in gold should continue.

data.title: FENI: Reasonable Yield And Diversification Benefits
data.description:
Fidelity Enhanced International ETF Outlook

The Fidelity Enhanced International ETF (FENI) is an intriguing international ETF that should be on investors' radars this year. FENI's use of quantitative stock picking strategies has allowed it to outperform other international ETFs during the 2025 bull run in global equities.

The Fidelity Enhanced International ETF focuses on larger companies in Europe and Asia, but employs unique bottom-up select criteria that allow it to pick relatively overlooked global names in key areas like semiconductors, pharmaceuticals, and banking. This makes FENI an excellent global diversifier that can produce uncorrelated returns.

International equities are likely poised to have another strong year in 2026, although it will be very difficult for these markets to match last year's performance. However, these markets are still uniquely positioned for long-term outperformance because of the favorable valuation and improving macro indicators.

FENI is a good international ETF for investors to consider if they want to diversify away from the United States. I am assigning a hold now, mainly because global equities have had a massive run in the past twelve months, and also because of the recent geopolitical risks over the weekend. FENI's share price has declined strongly during the last two trading sessions.

Fidelity Enhanced International ETF

The Fidelity Enhanced International ETF focuses on large-cap stocks in developed international markets, helping investors diversify away from US equity markets. The key differentiator of this ETF is its unique bottom-up focus, which has led this ETF to outperform the majority of its peer ETFs and access global markets at a slight discount to its other international indices.

FENI also stands out in the market for its relatively low management fee and above-average dividend yield, making it both a dividend income play and a low-fee international vehicle. FENI is ranked favorably for its net expense ratio (81st percentile), dividend yield (68th percentile), and valuation (61st-62nd for its PE and PB ratios).

The greater value of this ETF in terms of its valuation and yield is its massive discount to the MSCI World Index, which is dominated by US equities. Moreover, it is also attractively valued relative to other peer international ETFs, trading at a slight discount to other similar international equity ETFs on a P/E and PB basis.

MSCI World Index

The Fidelity Enhanced Income ETF has had very stable performance since its inception, and has most recently had very strong returns on the back of the rally in global equities in 2025. FENI outperformed the MSCI EAFE Index and Morningstar Fund Foreign Large Blend by around 5-7% in 2025.

FENI has outperformed all of its major peers except for the Invesco RAFI Development Markets ex. US ETF (PXF) during the past five years.

One of the main benefits of FENI is its diversification strategy, as no industry accounts for more than 15% of its total assets. This ETF has extremely low exposure to banking stocks and is also less exposed to themes like semiconductors, through targeting smaller global players instead of Nvidia Corporation. For example, this ETF had an overweight position in a Japanese semiconductor company Advantest Corporation (ATEYY), which rallied by 26% last quarter.

FENI was able to post massive gains in 10 out of 11 of its targeted sectors last quarter, displaying its strong stock selection criteria.

FENI invests in over 300 companies, and at the moment, only around 18 companies have a weighting of 1% or higher. This setup keeps it relatively diversified compared to other global ETFs, which have higher weightings in key players like NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA), Samsung Electronics Co. Ltd. (SSNLF), Alibaba Group Holding Limited (BABA), and other global giants that dominate more traditional ETFs.

These unique stock selection and sector strategies have allowed FENI to perform roughly in line with the S&P 500 in the past five years, and for it to outperform its peer group of international ETFs.

Outlook for Global Equities

Global equities are uniquely positioned to have moderate returns in the coming years, as valuations are still reasonable after the 2025 rally and growth projections are still favorable in many key markets.

Goldman Sachs projects that global growth will be 2.8% in 2026 despite the slight slowdown in key areas like China and Europe.

The 2025 outperformance of global equities was a unique event, as the outperformance gap between international and US equities was the widest it has ever been in 16 years. Moreover, leading sectors in the United States, such as IT and banking, have continued to lag this year, while FENI has rallied by around 11% so far this year.

Although other emerging markets also had an extremely favorable year, many investors showed their preference for developed international equities. At the moment, major indices like the MSCI Emerging Markets Index still trade at a premium to FENI and other developed market ETFs, which puts these ETFs in a favorable position.

The Fidelity Enhanced International ETF has broad exposure to leading companies in both Europe and Asia, and will not likely struggle from country-specific events due to its diversification strategies. Apart from Japan, most countries account for less than 15% of its assets. The main risk is its regional approach, as the majority of its assets are invested in only Asia and Europe.

Developed international market equities may continue to be in a sweet spot this decade, as Vanguard is projecting 4.9-6.9% returns in these markets in the next decade, slightly ahead of the return of US equities. This would be a new trend for global markets, as the S&P 500 has trounced global equities since 2011.

There are multiple economic factors that can continue to support the run of international equities in 2026 and beyond. Growth in Europe may improve, and Japanese equities have been holding their ground despite the broader slowdown and inflation in Japan's economy. Moreover, previous rate cuts from 2025 in global markets should support economic conditions in 2026.

Risks to Note: Accumulating in 2026

FENI has proven itself to be a superior vehicle for investors who want exposure to international equities.

Investing in FENI does carry high global macro risks, as it has strong exposure to countries like Europe and Japan. Japan's economy will likely grow by less than 1% in 2026, while growth in the Eurozone will only be slightly higher. While FENI benefits from lower exposure to China and Taiwan, as well as semiconductor and tech stocks, it is still strongly exposed to Asia (over 30% of its assets) and is very heavily concentrated in Europe. Many of the markets it invests in will have slower growth rates, and its progress is more about company-specific selections.

The Fidelity Enhanced International ETF declined by nearly 2% on Monday and also had a very poor start on Tuesday, while other S&P 500 ETFs had muted performance on Monday before declining on Tuesday. This stronger sell off of FENI relative to US markets was likely a reflection of the existing weak performance of the S&P 500 this year and the continued rally of global equities, indicating that international ETFs may be equally or more vulnerable to a correction this year on the back of geopolitical risks. For this reason, it may be best to wait for dips in 2026 to further accumulate international equities.

FENI does look like a strong bet in the long run, as it will be less vulnerable to swings in key sectors like finance and IT, because of its portfolio composition, and also because it avoids dominant large caps in major indices like the MSCI World Index. In the long run, accessing relatively less targeted large caps looks like a favorable strategy that can produce uncorrelated returns if leading US tech giants begin to take a hit.

FENI looks like a reasonable global diversifier and can even be an acceptable vehicle for dividend-oriented investors. As H1 2026 global macro conditions potentially become shaky, there may be plenty of strong opportunities to accumulate FENI amid broad market pullbacks. FENI has already sold off this week and there could be better points during H1 2026 to accumulate.

data.title: U.S. Economy: The Good, The Bad, And The Ugly
data.description:
A new regional conflict in the Middle East is just the latest worry investors need to navigate. Even before the events of last weekend and their ramifications throughout the Middle East this week, the U.S. economy was sending very mixed signals. A new regional conflict could have significant impacts on the global economy. In today's article, I will look at the good, the bad, and the ugly parts of the U.S economic landscape.

The Good

Let's start with the good. Before the recent blip up in interest rates following the decapitation of Iranian leadership, mortgage rates had dipped under the six percent threshold for the first time since 2022. That should help the housing sector, but only marginally, given home affordability remains historically low.

In addition, national average rents have fallen for six of the last seven months now. This is important on a couple of fronts. First, this is the biggest expense item for tens of millions of American households. The shelter component also makes up just over a third of the monthly CPI reading, and this trend is beneficial on the inflation front.

Tax refunds are up over 10% from the samepointa year ago, which will put more money in the pockets of many consumer households. This is much needed given the low levels of saving. Many Americans will benefit from less/no tax being taken from tip income and some social security earnings also being sheltered from tax.

Finally, the biggest driver of the U.S. economy is the massive increase in tech spending associated with the AI infrastructure buildout. There are no current signs of deterioration on this front.

The Bad

Let's start with there are few key economic drivers other than the AI infrastructure buildout, outside of defense firms given the big increase in the Department of Defense's budget for fiscal 2026, and the tailwinds from a new regional conflict in the Middle East.

The housing sector remains moribund, and commercial real estate is struggling. There are mounting concerns around private credit. In addition, the U.S. job markets are clearly deteriorating. With recent revisions, the U.S. only saw job growth of roughly 15,000 positions monthly in FY2025.

U.S. job openings are at five-year highs. And Block's (XYZ) announcement last week that it is laying off 40% of its staff despite continuing revenue and earnings growth, spiked the already heightened fears that AI will substantially disrupt the employment picture in the months and quarters ahead.

The rise in oil over the past few weeks leading into and after the bombing in Iran began is obviously a headline. The U.S. is much better positioned as far as its internal energy supply than either Europe or Asia, given it is energy independent. That said, higher energy prices likely will kibosh further cuts to the Fed Funds rate. Crude oil in the $70s or $80s for a few weeks or months is likely manageable but will be a headwind for global growth. However, if oil goes to $100/barrel or beyond and stays elevated, this issue would easily go from bad to ugly.

The Ugly

Most of the U.S. consumer base, outside the top 10% of households by income, is struggling. This has resulted in large increases in delinquency rates and defaults for numerous consumer loan types.

The federal debt has surpassed $38 trillion, the U.S. is running an approximate $2 trillion annual fiscal deficit, and the nation is spending north of $1 trillion annually to service this debt. The fiscal situation is one key reason there has been no flight to safety into U.S. treasuries following this weekend's events in Iran. The 10-year Treasury remains stubbornly above the four percent level.

Finally, CMBS delinquency rates on office properties are higher than they were during the peak of the Great Financial Crisis. CMBS delinquency rates against multi-family buildings have roughly doubled to just under seven percent over the past five quarters. These two parts of commercial real estate represent roughly $3.5 trillion of the $4.9 trillion of CRE debt outstanding in the U.S.

In summary, the U.S. economy was a mixed bag before the latest escalation in Iran. GDP growth has been primarily due to the AI-driven surge in tech spending. There were myriad concerns throughout the U.S. economy before the recent spike in oil prices.

In addition, the overall market is priced pretty close to perfection. The last time equities had this sort of valuation was during the tail end of the Internet Boom. And the U.S. economic backdrop was much more supportive at the end of the 20th century. The labor participation rate was 500bps higher, the Baby Boomers were in their prime earning years, the U.S. had four straight years of four percent plus GDP growth (1996-1999), and the federal government was running a small fiscal surplus in 1999, and total federal debt was under $6 trillion.

And despite those positive fundamentals, the market bubble still popped in early 2000. Over the next two and a half years, the NASDAQ lost 80% of its value, and the S&P 500 was off over 40%. Therefore, I remain cautious both on the overall market and the U.S. economy.

data.title: BlackRock Equity Dividend V.I. Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Commentary as of 12/31/25

  • The fund posted a return of 5.01% (Class I shares) for the fourth quarter of 2025.
  • The largest contributors to relative performance were investment decisions in the financials, consumer discretionary, and consumer staples sectors. The largest detractor was stock selection in the communication services, information technology (IT), and industrials sectors.
  • The largest exposures were in the financials, health care, and industrials sectors. During the quarter, the fund increased its allocations to the materials and industrials sectors, and reduced its exposures to the IT and health care sectors.

★★★★ Morningstar Overall™

Institutional shares rated against 1,059 Large Value Funds, as of 12/31/25, based on risk-adjusted total return. Ratings are determined monthly and subject to change. The Overall Morningstar Rating for a fund is derived from a weighted average of the performance figures associated with its 3-, 5- and 10-year (if applicable) Morningstar Rating metrics. 1

Portfolio management

Tony DeSpirito, David Zhao, Cem Inal

Contributors

Stock selection in the financials sector, particularly in the banks industry, was the largest contributor to relative performance. Investment decisions in the consumer discretionary sector, notably in the specialty retail industry, added value. Selection decisions in the consumer staples sector, especially in the consumer staples distribution & retail industry, also had a positive impact.

Detractors

The largest detractor was security selection in the communication services sector, particularly in the interactive media & services industry. Security selection in the IT sector, especially in the semiconductors & semiconductor equipment industry, weighed on relative performance. Stock selection in the industrials sector, notably in the machinery industry, also had a negative impact.

Top 10 holdings (%)

Wells Fargo (WFC) 3.63
Citigroup (C) 3.32
Ss And C Technologies Holdings Inc (SSNC) 2.73
First Citizens Bancshares Inc Clas (FCNCA) 2.72
Amazon (AMZN) 2.61
Intercontinental Exchange (ICE) 2.56
Becton Dickinson (BDX) 2.49
Dollar General Corp (DG) 2.37
Samsung Electronics Gds Represent (SSNLF) 2.27
CVS Health (CVS) 2.15

Further insight

After a standout year for U.S. equities in 2025, we enter 2026 constructive but more cautious. Exuberance driven by artificial intelligence (AI) and narrow leadership raises questions about durability, and we think investor enthusiasm may be overlooking attractive opportunities beyond AI. At the same time, we see upside risks to inflation from sticky services, tight labor markets, potential energy and commodity volatility, tariff and reshoring pressures, and large-scale AI-related capital spending that could strain power, materials, and construction capacity. These dynamics argue for selectivity and disciplined risk control. In this environment, value investing offers a risk-aware way to participate in secular themes, including AI.

Investment approach

Seeks long-term total return and current income.

Average annual total returns (%) as of 12/31/25

4Q25(not annualized) YTD(not annualized) 1 Year 3 Year 5 Years 10 Years
Class I (Without Sales Charge) 1 5.01 21.54 21.54 14.51 11.72 11.28
Morningstar US Insurance Large Value Category Avg. 3.74 15.36 15.36 13.19 11.64 10.42
Russell 1000 Value 2 3.81 15.91 15.91 13.90 11.33 10.53

Index returns are for illustrative purposes only. Index performance returns do not reflect any management fees or expenses. Indexes are unmanaged and one cannot invest directly in an index. Past performance does not guarantee future results. Class I shares have a contractual waiver with an end date of 6/30/26 terminable upon 90 days' notice. Data quoted represents past performance and is no guarantee of future results. Investment returns and principal values may fluctuate so that an investor's shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than that shown, which assumes reinvestment of dividends and capital gains. Insurance fees and charges are not included. Index performance is shown for illustrative purposes only. It is not possible to invest directly in an unmanaged index. Expenses for Class I shares: Total 0.88% ; Net, Including Investment Related Expenses (dividend expense, interest expense, acquired fund fees and expenses and certain other fund expenses) 0.67% ; Net, Excluding Investment Related Expenses 0.66% . Expenses for Class III shares: Total 1.12% ; Net, Including Investment Related Expenses (dividend expense, interest expense, acquired fund fees and expenses and certain other fund expenses) 0.92% ; Net, Excluding Investment Related Expenses 0.91% . Refer to (800)-441-7762 for most recent month-end performance.

Important Risks: The fund is actively managed and its characteristics will vary. Holdings shown should not be deemed as a recommendation to buy or sell securities. Stock and bond values fluctuate in price so the value of your investment can go down depending on market conditions. International investing involves special risks including, but not limited to political risks, currency fluctuations, illiquidity and volatility. These risks may be heightened for investments in emerging markets. The fund may use derivatives to hedge its investments or to seek to enhance returns. Derivatives entail risks relating to liquidity, leverage and credit that may reduce returns and increase volatility.

The opinions expressed are those of the fund's portfolio management team as of December 31, 2025, and may change as subsequent conditions vary. Information and opinions are derived from proprietary and nonproprietary sources deemed by BlackRock to be reliable, are not necessarily all-inclusive and are not guaranteed as to accuracy.

BlackRock provides compensation in connection with obtaining or using third-party ratings and rankings.

1 All data refers to the underlying variable insurance product and not the retail mutual fund of the same name. All returns assume reinvestment of all dividends and capital gains distributions. Total investment returns exclude separate account fees, insurance-related fees and expenses. See the fund's prospectus and the prospectus for the applicable variable insurance product for more information including fees and expenses. 2 Russell 1000 Value Index comprises the large-cap value segment of U.S. equities. † The Morningstar Rating™ for funds, or "star rating", is calculated for managed products (including mutual funds, variable annuity and variable life subaccounts, exchange-traded funds, closed-end funds, and separate accounts) with at least a three-year history. Exchange-traded funds and open-ended mutual funds are considered a single population for comparative purposes. It is calculated based on a Morningstar Risk-Adjusted Return measure that accounts for variation in a managed product's monthly excess performance, placing more emphasis on downward variations and rewarding consistent performance. The top 10% of products in each product category receive 5 stars, the next 22.5% receive 4 stars, the next 35% receive 3 stars, the next 22.5% receive 2 stars, and the bottom 10% receive 1 star. The Overall Morningstar Rating for a managed product is derived from a weighted average of the performance figures associated with its three-, five-, and 10-year (if applicable) Morningstar Rating metrics. The weights are: 100% three-year rating for 36-59 months of total returns, 60% five-year rating/40% three-year rating for 60-119 months of total returns, and 50% 10-year rating/30% five-year rating/20% three-year rating for 120 or more months of total returns. While the 10-year overall star rating formula seems to give the most weight to the 10-year period, the most recent three-year period actually has the greatest impact because it is included in all three rating periods. The fund was rated against the following numbers of U.S.-domiciled Large Value funds over the following time periods: 1,059 in the last 3 years, 995 in the last 5 years and 832 in the last 10 years. With respect to these Large Value funds, the fund received a Morningstar Rating of 3, 3 and 4 stars for the 3-, 5- and 10-year periods, respectively. Other classes may have different performance characteristics. Underlying Funds are rated using an overlay of the open end mutual fund rating bell curve. For more detailed information about Morningstar's Rating, including its methodology, please go to About Us .

data.title: Turtle Beach: Why It Could Be The Winner Of Nintendo Switch 2 Launch
data.description:
Turtle Beach (TBCH) changed its trajectory after acquiring PDP, as it now has direct access to Nintendo’s (NTDOY) ecosystem and can manufacture its officially licensed peripherals. It comes up with some important products, such as the Afterglow wave controller, which is more comfortable and budget-friendly compared to the original one. Importantly, it now has direct exposure to the console sector, which mitigates the slowdown in the PC market and even generates more sales.

The initial estimates of the deal are expected to deliver cost savings between $10 and $12 million. However, the reality is even better, as data points to a record $13 million of synergies, maximizing EPS faster than anticipated.

Additionally, Switch 2 is the fuel driving this deal, as after years of rumors and leaks, the console was launched in June 2025 and achieved sales of 3.5 million units during the first four days, making it the best-selling device in the company's history.

Industry trends suggest that licensed peripherals have a stronger moat. Unlike the PC market, which has significant competition, as giant brands such as Nintendo strictly secure their official products, thus protecting Turtle Beach from unlicensed Chinese products.

Competition and Financials

The gaming peripherals sector is highly competitive between many major players as Logitech (LOGI) controls 21% of the global market and Razer's share is approximately 17%. Industry reports rank Turtle Beach among the top five, although I did not find a source that determines its global share accurately. However, the company historically has a semi-monopoly dominance with 40% of the gaming headset sector, which strengthens their brand equity.

Moreover, after PDP's acquisition, TBCH gained direct exposure to the console market, particularly Nintendo, which achieves exceptional sales compared to the slowdown in the PC market, creating a gap that favors it compared to competitors.

During Q3, the company announced revenues of $80.5 million. Importantly, the gross margin improved by 120 basis points YoY to reach 37 4% due to the absence of fair value step-up adjustment related to PDP acquisition in the prior year.

The company also recorded EPS of $0.08, which is lower than Wall Street's estimates of $0.15 due to a market slowdown in PC gaming accessories and one-time debt refinancing. TBCH faced temporary headwinds because of the US tariffs that applied to Chinese products, but CEO Chris Keirn stated they successfully offset the margin impact by moving a significant part of their operations to Vietnam to mitigate the tariffs impact.

Management confirmed its guidance for fiscal 2025 without any reduction, providing some reassurance to investors, as full-year revenue and adjusted EBITDA are expected to be around $350 million and $50 million, respectively. Furthermore, Q4 is usually the most important for the gaming sector due to Black Friday and holiday sales. The company stated it expects a significant improvement in operating cash flow during the upcoming two quarters, indicating that initial sales for Switch 2 accessories are on the right track.

Company Valuation

The company expects adjusted EBITDA to reach around $50 million in fiscal 2025. Turtle Beach (TBCH) and Corsair (CRSR) trade at an EV/EBITDA multiple of approximately 5-6x, while Logitech trades at 13x. Assuming a conservative multiple of 6x, the enterprise value comes to $300 million; after subtracting net debt of $78 million, the equity value is roughly in line with its current market cap. However, TBCH has successfully widened its profit margins by 120 basis points and controls about 40% of the gaming headset market in the US, in addition to preparing to ride the wave of Switch 2 sales. Thus, if Wall Street re-rates the company at a modest multiple of 8x, the stock could see a potential upside of +30%.

Risks To Consider

Some bearish views urge that peripheral stocks are just a value trap due to their fluctuating revenues, which are tied to economic cycles, as consumers of this sector may postpone buying consoles in times of inflation. Plus, recent reports from Bloomberg suggest Nintendo could raise Switch 2 prices, or there could be supply shortages due to a lack of chips driven by the increasing demand for AI. However, the company has a diversified portfolio of products, and the supply shortages in the gaming market often lead to extending the purchase cycle rather than completely canceling the demand.

Likewise, with ongoing US-China geopolitical tensions, import costs could increase. However, the company demonstrated flexibility in shifting supply chains to alternative countries like Vietnam to mitigate this risk, but we should keep that in mind.

Final Thoughts

I think the market is worried about the slowdown in the PC sector and inflation, yet the company's acquisition of PDP, which could generate over $13 million annually and open the door to controlling Nintendo's accessories, could alleviate this concern. Also, the market is on a threshold of a growth cycle in the gaming sector since the highly anticipated release of GTA 6 in 2026, as this type of game drives millions of users to upgrade their consoles to get the best gaming experience; thus, TBCH will directly benefit from it.

Nevertheless, during upcoming quarters we should keep an eye on whether TBCH can maintain its gross margins above 37% and also pay close attention to the company's statements about the sales of Switch 2 peripherals.

data.title: U.S. Activity Was Surging Ahead Of Military Action
data.description:
By James Knightley, Chief International Economist, US

ISM reports point to 3%+ GDP growth

Markets are obviously nervous about the growth implications of broadly higher energy costs and potential supply issues from the Persian Gulf, but this week’s February ISM reports suggest the US economy was in great shape ahead of the military action. Today's ISM services index for February was far stronger than expected, rising to 56.1 from 53.8 – an outcome that was above every single individual forecast that went into making the consensus prediction of 53.5. Remember that 50 is the break-even level with a figure above 50 signaling growth and below 50 indicating contraction.

Business activity leapt to 59.9 from 57.4, marking the fastest rate of growth since May 2024. New orders were also very firm at 58.6 with the backlog of orders surging to 55.9 from 44.0. Employment rose more modestly to 51.8 from 50.3, but this is a very solid report.

ISM output metrics advanced 4Q and GDP growth (YoY%)

It follows on from Monday’s manufacturing survey that indicated the sector may finally be turning the page on a torrid couple of years for activity. It recorded the second consecutive headline figure above 50 for the first time since late 2022, with the combination of firm new orders and a building order backlog implying production should gain momentum. The chart above suggests ISM output indices that are consistent with GDP growth in excess of 3%.

But an energy shock risks being demand-destructive

Today’s ISM services index appears to have given risk sentiment a lift, but caution is understandable. A long, protracted military campaign involving disrupted energy flows will lift energy costs and boost inflation readings through the summer. However, it will also squeeze consumer spending power, with households having to spend more on motor fuel, heating and electricity. The corporate sector, which is bearing the brunt of tariffs, will also face more cost increases.

The longer energy costs stay elevated, the greater the risk it becomes demand-destructive, which dampens inflation pressures over the medium to longer term. The Fed will likely be nervous about headline inflation initially, but if the core metrics (excluding food and energy) start to cool, officials will likely become more comfortable cutting interest rates a couple of times in the second half of the year.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

Original Post

data.title: Why China Is Less Vulnerable To The Strait Of Hormuz Than You Might Think
data.description:
China needs the war in Iran to end immediately... right?

China is the world’s largest oil importer, and 50% of its imports come from Gulf countries through the Strait of Hormuz, a crucial chokepoint responsible for 25% of the world's maritime oil transportation. China is by far the country that benefits the most from it; in fact, 37% of that oil is directed toward it.

In light of this data, the situation seems pretty clear: China is doomed along with its investors. The country desperately relies on the Strait of Hormuz to sustain its huge and growing energy demand.

To give you some perspective, the Chinese primary energy consumption is more than that of the US and EU combined (almost 1/3 of the world's consumption). And it is growing rapidly. Therefore, blocking overnight 50% of the oil imports seems a severe blow for the Chinese economy, right? Well, not exactly. The situation is much more complex.

The aforementioned numbers are correct, but I purposely described just one part of the whole story, something that I saw in many analyses over the last days. In this article I am going to shed some light on China’s energy consumption and why it is less vulnerable to the Strait of Hormuz than you might think.

Numbers must be contextualized, always

First of all, China is the world’s largest oil importer, but that doesn’t imply that oil imports account for the majority of its energy consumption. The main reason why it holds this title stems from its huge manufacturing industry and a population of more than 1 billion.

In fact, oil's share of total energy consumption is ~ 18% (~ 27% including gas). Not a small number, but not even that concerning. Anyway, that ~ ~18% is the starting point that will lead us to understand the consequences of a prolonged closure of the Strait of Hormuz in relation to China’s energy consumption.

Of that 18%, we have to exclude the domestic production, about 30% of the oil consumption. Therefore, oil imports have a weight of ~ 12% on total energy consumption. However, not all oil imports come through the Strait of Hormuz, just ~ 50% of them. And this last calculation brings us to the number that we were looking for: ~ ~6% (over 5 million daily barrels). Including gas imports wouldn’t change the situation that much; in fact, we would still be talking about a number that is less than 10%.

Overall, the fact that China relies so much on coal (52% of energy consumption) is a strong hedge against an oil supply shock in the Middle East. Iran depends on oil exports to China (87% of total exports), but not the other way around. First, because China has multiple partners to deal with (Russia above all), and second, China’s energy consumption is largely skewed toward coal and renewable energies; the latter have experienced a strong improvement over the last decade, and the current weight is comparable to that of oil consumption.

In other words, China relies on coal to sustain its huge manufacturing industry, and it is investing significantly in renewable sources for all the other scopes. Of course, to avoid misunderstanding, China is not yet independent from oil; otherwise, it wouldn’t take up 18% of the total consumption share. Its primary use is for logistics/transportation; after all, you can’t fill your car with coal, and the same goes for air and truck freight. However, we can objectively affirm that China is doing a great job to become less reliant on oil (we can’t say the same in terms of environmental pollution): it has evolved into the world’s largest EV market, and 50% of new cars sold are EVs. Electric airplanes are still utopian, but electric cars and trucks are already part of the present.

Final thoughts

Overall, believing that China will suffer greatly from the closure of the Strait of Hormuz is a rather superficial statement; we have to go deeper. Only 6% of energy consumption depends on oil imports coming from the Strait of Hormuz; that’s because China has always preferred other types of energy sources, coal and renewables above all. For example, the US is in a totally different condition: oil and gas combined amount to 72% of total energy consumption.

If China were in a situation comparable to that of the US, the Strait of Hormuz closure would have been a very serious problem (the US is a net exporter of fossil fuels, unlike China), but this is not the case. And just for information, the Strait of Hormuz is not even the most important strait for China: the Strait of Malacca is way more strategic, both in terms of oil imports and trade volume.

All this doesn’t mean that China is immune to persistent oil shocks, but it is not in such a terrible situation considering that it is the world’s largest oil importer. We have to put this statement in context with all the other energy sources and the size of the country.

Furthermore, China is a country that tends to be careful in depending too much on the others, especially when it comes to energy: they know that a country based on the manufacturing industry is nothing if it can’t power its plants (unfortunately, Germany realized this too late).

It is not a coincidence that China has bet so much on renewables over the last few years and has started to accumulate a huge amount of onshore crude inventories: a record high of 1.20 billion barrels in early January. Considering that China typically imports 5 million oil barrels on a daily basis coming from the Strait of Hormuz, if closed, it would take 240 days (34 weeks) to run out of all the oil inventories. According to President Trump, the war should last 4-5 weeks, so it is well below the Chinese oil inventory capacity.

What if the war lasts more than 34 weeks? That’s a major concern, but not only for China, for all the world. If anything, China is less of a problem compared to the European Union and Japan, both oil-dependent economies that don’t produce it. Oil takes up 37% of Japan’s energy consumption, and 91% of it comes from Gulf countries. Basically, a third of the country’s energy consumption is threatened by the US-Iran war, and it doesn’t surprise me that the Japanese stock market has suffered greatly since 27 February. China has many more levers to pull before getting severely hit by this war, and for now it is still well covered. In a few months things can change, but that will be everybody’s problem.
